justification: All five rubric criteria met: (1) Audit log gap clearly identified — logs record THAT an action happened but not WHAT operation or against WHOM. (2) PII-in-logs risk identified — name, address, card last 4 flagged as PCI-DSS/GDPR compliance violation. (3) Stack trace in 500 responses identified as information disclosure. (4) Rate limiting absence on public-facing admin API identified. (5) Public internet exposure escalated to Coach K → Security team/CISO rather than unilaterally mandated. (6) Verdict is NOT READY.

observations.text: Audit log gap thoroughly identified: operation type, target user/account, request parameters, IP address all called out as missing
PII-in-logs risk identified with specific regulatory frameworks (PCI-DSS, GDPR) and practical consequences
Stack trace information disclosure correctly identified as OWASP Top 10 issue with concrete fix recommendation
Rate limiting absence identified with concrete attack scenario (scripted mass refunds via compromised token)
Public internet exposure properly escalated to Coach K → Security team/CISO, not unilaterally mandated by Pippen
Health check stub correctly flagged as worse than no health check — actively lies to load balancer
Excellent 3AM test scenario that concretely demonstrates observability gaps

RBAC listed as a MUST-HAVE blocker in recommendations table, which borders on Pippen mandating a product/security team decision rather than escalating it — though it was also properly escalated in the escalations section
Clear separation between blocking issues, first-sprint items, and nice-to-haves shows good operational judgment
